title: Curve Sketching Exit Card
methodology: DeepSeek API generation + local validator/renderer QA

lessonAnalysis.coreLearningGoal: Students will be able to apply the curve sketching algorithm (domain, intercepts, asymptotes, first/second derivative analysis) to sketch polynomial and rational functions.
successCriteria: Determine domain and continuity of a function.
Identify vertical, horizontal, and oblique asymptotes for rational functions.
Compute first derivative, find critical points, and determine intervals of increase/decrease.
Compute second derivative, find inflection points, and determine concavity intervals.
Synthesize information to sketch an accurate graph.
lessonExamplesDetected: f(x) = x^2/(x-1) (rational function with oblique asymptote)
Matching graphs of f, f', f''
targetProducts: A fully labeled sketch of a function showing intercepts, asymptotes, local extrema, inflection points, and correct increasing/decreasing/concavity behavior.
inputEvidenceStudentsShouldUse: Sign charts for f' and f''
doNotRevealAsQuestionCondition: The exact coordinates of all points from the worked example

problemAnchor: A student is given a function and must produce a complete curve sketch. They need to systematically apply the algorithm to avoid missing key features.

abt.and: Students have learned individual calculus concepts (derivatives, limits)
abt.but: They struggle to integrate these into a coherent curve sketching process, often skipping steps or misinterpreting derivative signs.
abt.therefore: We need a diagnostic that checks their ability to execute each step of the algorithm and connect the results to the graph.

objective: Diagnose students' ability to apply the curve sketching algorithm to a new polynomial function, identifying domain, intercepts, critical points, intervals of increase/decrease, concavity, and inflection points.

knowledgeNodes.name: Domain and Continuity
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'find the domain... Is the function continuous? Is it not continuous? ... polynomial function ... continuous anywhere or everywhere.'

knowledgeNodes.name: Intercepts
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'find the intercepts... set the function y f equal to 0, and then when we factor, we find our roots'

knowledgeNodes.name: Asymptotes
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'find any asymptotes. If the function is not continuous... vertical asymptote is 1, set the denominator equal to 0... oblique asymptote... use long division'

knowledgeNodes.name: First Derivative Test
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'first derivative test... determine if a function is increasing or decreasing... find the critical points, then we check the interval chart... sign chart'

knowledgeNodes.name: Second Derivative Test and Concavity
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'second derivative test... analyze where the function is concave up or concave down... sign chart for F double prime... identify possible points of inflection'

knowledgeNodes.name: Inflection Points
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'points of inflection, where the first derivative is equal to 0, the second derivative is equal to 0... gives us the turning points'

knowledgeNodes.name: Synthesizing Sketch
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher: 'sketch the whole function... putting it all together... summarize all the local extreme points of inflection... and then we sketch the graph'

errorTaxonomy.meaning: Incorrect domain identification (e.g., missing restrictions for rational/root functions, or assuming discontinuity for polynomials).
Algebraic errors in finding intercepts (e.g., factoring mistakes, missing x=0 as intercept).
Misidentifying asymptotes (e.g., confusing vertical/horizontal/oblique, incorrect long division, missing asymptote due to degree comparison).
Errors in first derivative computation or critical point classification (e.g., derivative mistakes, missing critical points where f' undefined, misinterpreting sign chart).
Errors in second derivative computation or concavity analysis (e.g., derivative mistakes, misinterpreting sign of f'', incorrect inflection point x-values).
Failure to connect derivative information to graph shape (e.g., sketching increase when f' negative, concave up when f'' negative).
Incomplete or inaccurate synthesis (e.g., missing key points, asymptotes not drawn, inconsistent behavior near asymptotes).

figures.title: Sign chart for f'(x) of a polynomial

answerLock.explanation: f'(x) > 0 for x < -1 and x > 2; f'(x) < 0 for -1 < x < 2.

figures.title: Sign chart for f''(x) of a polynomial

answerLock.explanation: f''(x) < 0 for x < 0.5 (concave down); f''(x) > 0 for x > 0.5 (concave up).

figures.title: Graphs of f, f', f'' for matching

labels.text: A: f has max near x=-1 and min near x=1
B: f' crosses x-axis at those extrema
C: f'' crosses x-axis where B has its minimum

answerLock.explanation: A has local extrema near x=-1 and x=1 where B crosses the x-axis. B has a local minimum near x=0 where C crosses the x-axis. Therefore B is f'.

questions.cognitiveAction: Identify the domain of a polynomial function.
questions.stem: What is the domain of the function f(x) = 2x^3 - 5x^2 + 3x - 7?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Polynomials are defined for all real numbers.
options.feedbackWrong: Polynomials have no restrictions; their domain is all real numbers.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: There is no division by x, so x=0 is allowed.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Polynomials are defined for negative x as well.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Polynomials are defined for all real numbers, including zero and negatives.

questions.cognitiveAction: Find the x-intercepts of a factored polynomial.
questions.stem: Given f(x) = x(x - 2)(x + 1), what are the x-intercepts?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Setting each factor to zero gives x=0, x=2, x=-1.
options.feedbackWrong: Set each factor equal to zero: x=0, x-2=0 → x=2, x+1=0 → x=-1.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Check the signs when solving x-2=0 and x+1=0.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Don't forget the factor x, which gives x=0.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: There are three factors, so three x-intercepts.

questions.cognitiveAction: Identify the vertical asymptote of a rational function.
questions.stem: Consider the function f(x) = (x+2)/(x-3). What is the equation of the vertical asymptote?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Vertical asymptotes come from the denominator, not the numerator.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Setting the denominator x-3=0 gives x=3.
options.feedbackWrong: Set the denominator equal to zero: x-3=0 → x=3.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: y=1 is a horizontal asymptote, not vertical.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: This is not an asymptote; check by setting the denominator to zero.

questions.cognitiveAction: Determine intervals of increase from a sign chart.
questions.stem: The sign chart for f'(x) is shown in Figure 1. On which interval(s) is f increasing?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: f is also increasing on (2, ∞) where f'(x) > 0.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: On (-1, 2), f'(x) < 0, so f is decreasing.

options.label: C
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! f'(x) > 0 on these intervals, so f is increasing.
options.feedbackWrong: Look for intervals where f'(x) is positive (+).

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: f is also increasing on (-∞, -1).

questions.cognitiveAction: Classify a critical point using the first derivative test.
questions.stem: Using Figure 1, what type of critical point occurs at x = -1?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: f' changes from + to -, which indicates a local maximum.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! f' changes from positive to negative, so f has a local maximum at x=-1.
options.feedbackWrong: Check the sign change: f' goes from + to -, so it's a local maximum.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: There is a sign change, so it is a local extremum.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Inflection points are determined by f'', not f'.

questions.cognitiveAction: Determine concavity from a sign chart.
questions.stem: The sign chart for f''(x) is shown in Figure 2. On which interval is f concave up?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: On (-∞, 0.5), f''(x) < 0, so f is concave down.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! f''(x) > 0 on (0.5, ∞), so f is concave up.
options.feedbackWrong: Concave up means f''(x) > 0; look for the + interval.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: The sign of f'' changes, so concavity is not the same everywhere.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: There is an interval where f'' is positive.

questions.cognitiveAction: Identify the x-coordinate of an inflection point from f'' sign change.
questions.stem: Using Figure 2, at which x-value does an inflection point occur?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Figure 2 is for f'', not f'. Inflection points occur where f'' changes sign.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: At x=0, f'' is negative on both sides? Check the chart.

options.label: C
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! f'' changes from negative to positive at x=0.5, indicating an inflection point.
options.feedbackWrong: Inflection points occur where f'' changes sign; that happens at x=0.5.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: x=2 is a critical point from Figure 1, not an inflection point from Figure 2.

questions.cognitiveAction: Match graphs of f, f', f'' based on derivative relationships.
questions.stem: Figure 3 shows three graphs labeled A, B, C. Which graph represents f'?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Graph A has local extrema where B crosses the x-axis, so A is likely f.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! B crosses the x-axis at the extrema of A, and its extrema align with C's x-intercepts, so B is f'.
options.feedbackWrong: Look for the graph that shows the slopes of another graph.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: C crosses the x-axis at the extrema of B, so C is likely f''.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: The relationship between the graphs can be determined by analyzing extrema and x-intercepts.

questions.cognitiveAction: Synthesize derivative evidence into graph-feature predictions without drawing the final graph.
questions.stem: Given f'(x) > 0 for x < -1 and x > 2, f'(x) < 0 for -1 < x < 2, f''(x) < 0 for x < 0.5, and f''(x) > 0 for x > 0.5, list the increasing/decreasing intervals, concavity intervals, and the likely x-values of local extrema and inflection. Do not draw the final graph.
questions.rubric: Strong answer identifies: increasing on (-∞,-1) and (2,∞); decreasing on (-1,2); concave down on (-∞,0.5); concave up on (0.5,∞); local maximum near x=-1; local minimum near x=2; inflection candidate at x=0.5 because f'' changes sign.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: f is increasing on (-∞,-1) and (2,∞), and decreasing on (-1,2). It is concave down on (-∞,0.5) and concave up on (0.5,∞). Since f' changes from positive to negative at x=-1, there is a local maximum there. Since f' changes from negative to positive at x=2, there is a local minimum there. Since f'' changes from negative to positive at x=0.5, x=0.5 is an inflection candidate.

questions.cognitiveAction: Explain the relationship between f, f', and f'' in curve sketching.
questions.stem: Explain how the signs of f'(x) and f''(x) determine the shape of the graph of f(x). Use the terms increasing, decreasing, concave up, and concave down in your explanation.
questions.rubric: Must state: f'(x) > 0 implies f increasing, f'(x) < 0 implies f decreasing; f''(x) > 0 implies concave up, f''(x) < 0 implies concave down. May mention critical points and inflection points.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The sign of f'(x) tells us whether f is increasing or decreasing: if f'(x) > 0, the graph goes up as x increases; if f'(x) < 0, it goes down. The sign of f''(x) tells us about concavity: if f''(x) > 0, the graph is concave up (curving upward like a cup); if f''(x) < 0, it is concave down (curving downward like a cap). Together, they determine the overall shape, such as increasing and concave down, or decreasing and concave up.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student does not recognize that polynomials have domain all real numbers, or incorrectly applies restrictions from rational/root functions to polynomials.

miniLesson.explain: The domain of a function is the set of all x-values for which the function is defined. Polynomial functions (sums of powers of x with real coefficients) are defined for all real numbers because you can plug any real number into a polynomial and get a real output. There are no division by zero or even roots of negative numbers to worry about.
miniLesson.example: f(x) = 3x^5 - 2x^2 + 7 is a polynomial. Its domain is all real numbers, written as (-∞, ∞) or ℝ.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Polynomials = no restrictions. Think: 'Poly wants all x's.'

guidedPractice.prompt: What is the domain of f(x) = x^2 + 4?
guidedPractice.answer: All real numbers.

guidedPractice.prompt: Is there any value of x that makes f(x) = 5x^3 - x + 1 undefined?
guidedPractice.answer: No, it's a polynomial, so it's defined for all real x.

guidedPractice.prompt: Explain why the domain of f(x) = 1/x is not all real numbers, but the domain of f(x) = x is all real numbers.
guidedPractice.answer: f(x)=1/x has division by x, so x cannot be 0. f(x)=x is a polynomial, so no division or roots, thus all real numbers are allowed.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student makes factoring errors or forgets to set y=0 to find x-intercepts, or sets x=0 incorrectly for y-intercept.

miniLesson.explain: To find x-intercepts, set y=0 and solve for x. Factor the polynomial if possible. Each factor gives an x-intercept. To find the y-intercept, set x=0 and evaluate f(0).
miniLesson.example: For f(x)=x(x-2)(x+1), set 0=x(x-2)(x+1). By zero product property, x=0, x-2=0 → x=2, x+1=0 → x=-1. So x-intercepts at 0, 2, -1. y-intercept: f(0)=0(0-2)(0+1)=0.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: x-intercepts: let y=0 and factor. y-intercept: let x=0.

guidedPractice.prompt: Find the x-intercepts of f(x) = (x-3)(x+4).
Find the y-intercept of f(x) = x^2 - 5x + 6.
A student says the x-intercepts of f(x)=x^2-4 are 2 and -2, but forgets to check. Are they correct? What is the y-intercept?

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student confuses vertical asymptotes (denominator=0) with x-intercepts, or incorrectly determines horizontal/oblique asymptotes by not comparing degrees.

miniLesson.explain: For rational functions f(x)=p(x)/q(x): Vertical asymptotes where q(x)=0 (and p(x)≠0). Horizontal asymptote: if degree(p) < degree(q), y=0; if equal, y=leading coeff ratio; if degree(p) > degree(q), no horizontal asymptote (but may have oblique). Oblique asymptote if degree(p) = degree(q)+1; find by polynomial long division.
miniLesson.example: f(x)=(x+2)/(x-3). Denominator zero at x=3 → vertical asymptote x=3. Degrees equal (1 and 1) → horizontal asymptote y=1/1=1.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Vertical: set bottom=0. Horizontal: compare degrees (BOB0, EATS DC, or no HA). Oblique: degree top = bottom+1.

guidedPractice.prompt: Find the vertical asymptote of f(x) = 1/(x-5).
Does f(x) = (2x)/(x^2+1) have a horizontal asymptote? If so, what is it?
guidedPractice.answer: Degree top=1, bottom=2, so y=0.

guidedPractice.prompt: Find all asymptotes of f(x) = (x^2+1)/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: Vertical: x=1. Degree top=2, bottom=1 → no horizontal, but oblique. Long division: x^2+1 divided by x-1 gives x+1 + 2/(x-1), so oblique asymptote y=x+1.

reteachModules.trigger: Errors in first derivative computation or critical point classification
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student makes mistakes in differentiation rules, algebra when solving f'(x)=0, or misinterprets the sign chart (e.g., thinking positive derivative means decreasing).

miniLesson.explain: First derivative f'(x) gives slope. Critical points where f'(x)=0 or undefined. Use a sign chart: test points in each interval between critical points. If f'(x)>0, f is increasing; if f'(x)<0, f is decreasing. A critical point is a local max if f' changes from + to -, local min if - to +.
miniLesson.example: For f(x)=x^3-3x, f'(x)=3x^2-3=3(x-1)(x+1). Critical points at x=-1,1. Sign chart: (-∞,-1): + (increasing); (-1,1): - (decreasing); (1,∞): + (increasing). So local max at x=-1, local min at x=1.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: f' sign = slope direction. + means up, - means down. Max: + to -. Min: - to +.

guidedPractice.prompt: Find f'(x) for f(x)=4x^3 - 2x.
The sign chart for f'(x) is: (-∞,0): -, (0,2): +, (2,∞): -. Classify the critical point at x=0.
guidedPractice.answer: f' changes from - to +, so local minimum at x=0.

guidedPractice.prompt: A student says f(x)=x^3 has a local maximum at x=0 because f'(0)=0. Explain the error.
guidedPractice.answer: f'(x)=3x^2, which is positive on both sides of 0, so no sign change; thus no local extremum at 0 (it's an inflection point).

reteachModules.trigger: Errors in second derivative computation or concavity analysis
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student incorrectly computes second derivative, misinterprets sign (e.g., f''>0 means concave down), or fails to find inflection points where f'' changes sign.

miniLesson.explain: Second derivative f''(x) tells concavity. f''(x)>0 → concave up (cup shape); f''(x)<0 → concave down (cap shape). Inflection points occur where f''(x)=0 or undefined AND concavity changes (sign of f'' changes).
miniLesson.example: f(x)=x^3-3x, f''(x)=6x. Set 6x=0 → x=0. Sign chart: (-∞,0): - (concave down); (0,∞): + (concave up). Concavity changes at x=0, so inflection point at (0,0).
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Second derivative: positive = smile (concave up), negative = frown (concave down). Inflection = smile changes to frown or vice versa.

guidedPractice.prompt: If f''(x) = 2, is f concave up or down?
guidedPractice.answer: Concave up (positive).

guidedPractice.prompt: Find the x-coordinate of any inflection point for f(x)=x^4-6x^2.
guidedPractice.answer: f''(x)=12x^2-12=12(x-1)(x+1). Set =0 → x=1,-1. Sign changes at both, so inflection points at x=1 and x=-1.

guidedPractice.prompt: A student claims f(x)=x^4 has an inflection point at x=0 because f''(0)=0. Are they correct?
guidedPractice.answer: No, f''(x)=12x^2, which is positive on both sides of 0, so concavity does not change (always concave up). No inflection point.

reteachModules.trigger: Failure to connect derivative information to graph shape
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student can compute derivatives but cannot translate sign information into the actual shape of the graph (e.g., drawing increasing when derivative is negative).

miniLesson.explain: The graph of f is a visual representation of all the derivative information. f' tells slope: positive f' means graph goes up as x increases; negative f' means graph goes down. f'' tells bending: positive f'' means graph curves upward (like a cup); negative f'' means curves downward (like a cap). Combine them: e.g., f'>0 and f''<0 means increasing but bending downwards (getting less steep).
miniLesson.example: If f'>0 and f''>0, graph is increasing and concave up (getting steeper). If f'<0 and f''<0, graph is decreasing and concave down (getting steeper downward). Sketch small segments to practice.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Think of driving a car: f' is speed (positive=forward), f'' is acceleration (positive=speed increasing).

guidedPractice.prompt: If f'(x) > 0, is the graph going up or down?
guidedPractice.answer: Up (increasing).

guidedPractice.prompt: Describe the shape of f on an interval where f' < 0 and f'' > 0.
guidedPractice.answer: Decreasing and concave up (slope negative but becoming less negative, graph curving upward).

guidedPractice.prompt: Sketch a small segment of a graph where f' > 0 and f'' < 0. Explain your sketch.
guidedPractice.answer: The segment should go upward (increasing) but bend downward (concave down), like the right side of an upside-down parabola.

reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Student may correctly find individual features but fails to combine them into a coherent graph, missing asymptotes, inconsistent behavior near asymptotes, or not labeling key points.

miniLesson.explain: Curve sketching is like assembling a puzzle. Start by drawing asymptotes as dashed lines. Plot intercepts and critical/inflection points. Then use sign charts to sketch the curve's direction and bending in each interval. Ensure the graph approaches asymptotes correctly (e.g., vertical asymptote: graph goes to ±∞; horizontal/oblique: graph approaches the line as x→±∞).
miniLesson.example: For f(x)=1/(x-2)+1, vertical asymptote x=2, horizontal y=1. Plot y-intercept (0,0.5). As x→2⁻, f(x)→-∞; as x→2⁺, f(x)→+∞. As x→±∞, f(x)→1. Sketch accordingly.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Asymptotes are boundaries the graph cannot cross (usually). Plot points, then connect with correct slope and concavity.

guidedPractice.prompt: What is the first thing you should draw when sketching a rational function?
guidedPractice.answer: Dashed lines for asymptotes.

guidedPractice.prompt: For f(x)=1/(x-1), describe the behavior as x approaches 1 from the left.
guidedPractice.answer: As x→1⁻, x-1 is a small negative number, so 1/(small negative) → -∞. The graph goes down towards negative infinity.

guidedPractice.prompt: A student sketches f(x)=(x^2-1)/(x-2) but forgets the oblique asymptote. What is the asymptote and how would it affect the sketch?
guidedPractice.answer: Long division gives y=x+2. The graph should approach this line as x→±∞. Without it, the end behavior will be incorrect.

questions.stem: What is the domain of f(x) = -4x^6 + 2x^3 - 9?
Explain why the domain of f(x) = √(x-2) is not all real numbers, but the domain of f(x) = x^2 - 2 is all real numbers.
questions.rubric: Must mention that square root requires non-negative radicand, so x-2≥0 → x≥2. Polynomial has no restrictions.

questions.stem: Find the x-intercepts of f(x) = (x+3)(x-1)(x-4).
Find the x- and y-intercepts of f(x) = x^2 - 7x + 10.
questions.rubric: x-intercepts: set 0=x^2-7x+10=(x-5)(x-2) → x=5,2. y-intercept: f(0)=10. Must show work.

questions.stem: What are the vertical and horizontal asymptotes of f(x) = (3x)/(x+2)?
Find all asymptotes of f(x) = (x^2 - 1)/(x + 1).
questions.rubric: Factor numerator: (x-1)(x+1)/(x+1) = x-1 for x≠-1. Hole at x=-1, no vertical asymptote. No horizontal/oblique since it simplifies to linear (but technically oblique y=x-1 with hole). Accept explanation of hole vs asymptote.

questions.stem: Given f'(x) = (x-1)(x+2), on which interval is f decreasing?
Find the critical points of f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x and classify each as local max, min, or neither.
questions.rubric: f'(x)=3x^2-12x+9=3(x-1)(x-3). Critical points x=1,3. Sign chart: (-∞,1): +; (1,3): -; (3,∞): +. So local max at x=1, local min at x=3.

questions.stem: For f(x) = x^3 - 3x, what is the concavity at x = -1?
Find the inflection point(s) of f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3.
questions.rubric: f''(x)=12x^2-24x=12x(x-2). Set =0 → x=0,2. Sign chart: (-∞,0): +; (0,2): -; (2,∞): +. Concavity changes at both, so inflection points at x=0 and x=2. Find y: f(0)=0, f(2)=16-32=-16. Points: (0,0) and (2,-16).

questions.stem: If f'(x) < 0 and f''(x) > 0, which graph shape is correct?
Sketch a graph that is increasing and concave down. Label a segment where this occurs.
questions.rubric: Graph must go upward (positive slope) but bend downward (like right side of upside-down parabola). Accept any reasonable sketch with labels.

questions.stem: When sketching f(x) = 1/(x-2) + 1, what is the behavior as x → 2⁺?
Sketch the graph of f(x) = (x-1)/(x+2). Label all asymptotes and intercepts.
questions.rubric: Vertical asymptote x=-2; horizontal asymptote y=1; x-intercept (1,0); y-intercept (0,-1/2). Graph should approach asymptotes correctly. Must label.

teacherEscalation.condition: If a student scores 0-4 on the diagnostic card, indicating full reteach is needed.
teacherEscalation.message: This student has not demonstrated sufficient understanding of the curve sketching algorithm. Consider one-on-one or small group instruction focusing on the foundational steps: domain, intercepts, and derivative sign charts. Use the reteach modules for each error type. Provide additional practice with polynomial functions before moving to rational functions.
